claim.statement: The COVID-19 pandemic has significant psychological impacts on university students in Jordan, leading to high levels of psychological distress.
claim.statement_location: Title, Abstract, and Objectives statements (paper title and abstract/introduction describing the aim to assess psychological distress among university students during the COVID-19 pandemic)

data.source: Online self-administered questionnaire (including sociodemographic items, Kessler Psychological Distress Scale K10, questions on coping activities and concerns)
data.access_details: Data available from the corresponding author upon request (as stated in the paper); not publicly archived in the manuscript
data.notes: Convenience sampling via social media (Facebook and WhatsApp) among seven student groups; majority undergraduates (84.8%); psychometric property reported for K10 (Cronbach's α = 0.88); limitations noted regarding generalizability due to sampling strategy.

method.description: An internet-based cross-sectional survey distributed via social media in May 2020 to university students in Jordan. Participants completed a self-administered questionnaire capturing sociodemographic data, the 10-item Kessler Psychological Distress Scale (K10), coping activities, medication use for coping, and primary concerns during the pandemic. Responses were scored and categorized into distress levels. Statistical analysis included descriptive statistics, non-parametric tests, Spearman correlation, and multinomial logistic regression using SPSS v23. Ethical approval and informed consent procedures were implemented.
method.steps: 1) Design questionnaire including sociodemographics, K10 items, coping and concern items; 2) Obtain ethical approval and prepare informed consent; 3) Recruit participants via social media (Facebook and WhatsApp) targeting seven student groups; 4) Administer online self-completed survey in May 2020; 5) Collect responses (N=381) and clean data; 6) Compute K10 total scores (range 10-50) and categorize into none/mild/moderate/severe distress; 7) Compute descriptive statistics and psychometric reliability (Cronbach's α); 8) Perform non-parametric tests and Spearman correlation (e.g., age vs K10); 9) Fit multinomial logistic regression to assess associations (e.g., gender, motivation for distance learning) with distress categories; 10) Interpret results and report findings.
method.models: Non-parametric statistical tests, Spearman correlation, multinomial logistic regression
method.outcome_variable: Psychological distress measured by Kessler Psychological Distress Scale (K10) total score and categorized distress levels (no distress 10-19, mild 20-24, moderate 25-29, severe 30-50).
method.independent_variables: Age, gender, motivation for distance learning, coping activities, smoking status, academic level, pre-existing psychiatric history, and other sociodemographic variables collected in the questionnaire.
method.control_variables: Sociodemographic variables included in multivariable/multinomial logistic regression models (paper reports inclusion of variables such as age, gender, motivation for distance learning; exact covariate list in regression model not fully enumerated in the summary).

results.summary: High levels of psychological distress were observed among the surveyed university students. The mean K10 score was high, and the majority of participants were classified with severe distress. Younger age was associated with higher distress. Over half of students reported no motivation for distance learning, which was inversely associated with distress. Females showed higher levels of distress on some measures. Common coping activities included increased social media use and spending time with family. A minority used medications to cope. The K10 tool showed strong internal consistency (Cronbach's α = 0.88).

numerical_results.direction: negative (younger age associated with higher distress)

numerical_results.effect_size: SD not stated separately for gender
numerical_results.p_value: not stated (comparison P = 0.57 for female vs male mean)

numerical_results.effect_size: SD not stated separately for gender
numerical_results.p_value: not stated (comparison P = 0.57 for female vs male mean)

numerical_results.direction: negative association (higher motivation associated with lower odds of severe distress)

metadata.original_paper_title: Is it Just About Physical Health? An Internet-Based Cross-Sectional Study Exploring the Psychological Impacts of COVID-19 Pandemic on University Students in Jordan Using Kessler Psychological Distress Scale
metadata.original_paper_data: Available from the corresponding author upon request (as stated in the paper)
